---
title: "36 · A2C: Advantage Estimation and Baseline Invariance"
subtitle: "The advantage A(s,a) = q(s,a) - v(s) replaces q; lower-variance estimator that is the basis of every modern AC method."
lesson-id: "mfrl.advantage-actor-critic"
tier: "mfrl"
prerequisites: ["mfrl.actor-critic-qac"]
unlocks: []
applied-domains: ["ai-ml"]
estimated-minutes: 30
status: "done"
---

::: {.preflight-check}
### Pre-flight check {.mt-0}

1. Can you state **QAC** ([Lesson 35](../35-actor-critic-qac/index.qmd))?
2. Did you prove the **baseline invariance** lemma in Lesson 33, S2 — that
   $\mathbb E_{a \sim \pi}[\nabla \log \pi(a \mid s)\, b(s)] = 0$?
3. Do you remember the relation between $v$ and $q$:
   $v_\pi(s) = \sum_a \pi(a \mid s) q_\pi(s, a)$?

If yes to all three, continue.
:::

## Touch

A widget runs two policy-gradient agents side by side on a simple task — a
2D MountainCar. Both are actor-critic. Both have the same hyperparameters.
The only difference: the **left** agent uses $\hat q(s, a)$ in the actor's
gradient (QAC); the **right** agent uses the *advantage*
$\hat A(s, a) = \hat q(s, a) - \hat v(s)$.

The reward curves on the right are visibly cleaner. Less zigzag, more
monotone climb. Faster convergence to a good policy.

::: {.lesson-beat}
Same task. Same network. Same step size. The only thing different is
*subtracting $\hat v(s)$* — and the variance drops sharply.
:::

## Wonder

1. Subtracting $v(s)$ from $q(s, a)$ removes a term that doesn't depend on
   $a$. Could that possibly change *which* action the gradient pushes us
   toward?
2. The widget shows lower variance — but is the gradient still unbiased?
3. What is special about $v_\pi(s)$ as a baseline, compared to (say) a
   constant or an arbitrary state function?

## Discover

### Problem 1 — recall baseline invariance

From Lesson 33, S2: for any function $b(s)$ of state alone,
$\mathbb E_{a \sim \pi(\cdot \mid s)}[\nabla_\theta \log \pi(a \mid s)\, b(s)] = 0$.
What does this imply about subtracting $b(s)$ from $q_\pi(s, a)$ inside the
policy gradient theorem?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Linearity of expectation.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\mathbb E[\nabla \log \pi (q_\pi - b)] = \mathbb E[\nabla \log \pi q_\pi] -
\mathbb E[\nabla \log \pi b] = \nabla J - 0 = \nabla J$. So **subtracting any
state-only baseline leaves the policy gradient exactly unchanged**. The
*expected* gradient is the same; only its variance can change.
</details>

### Problem 2 — the optimal baseline is $v_\pi$

If we choose $b(s) = v_\pi(s)$, what does $q_\pi(s, a) - v_\pi(s)$ measure?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Recall $v_\pi(s) = \mathbb E_{a \sim \pi(\cdot \mid s)}[q_\pi(s, a)]$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$A_\pi(s, a) := q_\pi(s, a) - v_\pi(s)$ is **how much better than average**
the action $a$ is in state $s$ — under the current policy. Positive: $a$
beats the average; negative: $a$ is worse than the average. This is the
**advantage function**.

$\sum_a \pi(a \mid s) A_\pi(s, a) = 0$ for every $s$, by construction.
</details>

### Problem 3 — variance reduction in numbers

Suppose $q_\pi(s, a)$ at a state $s$ takes values close to 100 across
actions, varying by ±2. Without baseline, $\nabla \log \pi \cdot q$ has
magnitude proportional to 100. With baseline $b = 100$, the magnitude
drops to ~2. By how much does the variance drop?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Variance scales with the square of the multiplier.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Variance of $\nabla \log \pi \cdot c$ scales like $c^2 \|\nabla \log \pi\|^2$.
Going from $c \approx 100$ to $c \approx 2$ drops variance by a factor of
$(100/2)^2 = 2500$. The baseline correction is *unreasonably* effective
when $q$ is large but flat across actions.
</details>

### Problem 4 — estimate $A$ from a TD error

In practice we don't know $v_\pi$ or $q_\pi$. We learn one critic
$\hat v(s; w)$ and use the TD error as an *advantage estimator*. Show that
the 1-step TD error
$\delta_t = r_t + \gamma \hat v(s_{t+1}; w) - \hat v(s_t; w)$
is, in expectation, an unbiased estimator of $A_\pi(s_t, a_t)$ when $\hat v
= v_\pi$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$\mathbb E[r_t + \gamma v_\pi(s_{t+1}) \mid s_t, a_t] = q_\pi(s_t, a_t)$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\mathbb E[\delta_t \mid s_t, a_t] = \mathbb E[r_t + \gamma v_\pi(s_{t+1})
\mid s_t, a_t] - v_\pi(s_t) = q_\pi(s_t, a_t) - v_\pi(s_t) = A_\pi(s_t,
a_t)$. So the **TD error is an unbiased estimate of the advantage** when the
critic is correct. This is the trick that lets A2C carry only one critic
network ($\hat v$, not $\hat q$) and still estimate $A$.
</details>

::: {.fun-fact}
**A3C** — the asynchronous variant of A2C — was the dominant deep-RL
algorithm of 2016–2018, dethroning DQN in many Atari benchmarks (Mnih et
al. 2016). The synchronous A2C followed shortly after with cleaner
performance and easier debugging. **PPO** (Schulman et al. 2017)
eventually replaced A2C as the default, but A2C is still the cleanest
classroom example of advantage estimation.
:::

::: {.key-insight}
**$A_\pi(s, a)$ answers "is this action better than *average* in this
state?"** — exactly what the policy gradient needs. Subtracting the
average is *free* variance reduction: the expectation is unchanged, the
variance can drop by orders of magnitude. Every modern actor-critic
algorithm uses some advantage-style estimator.
:::

## Name

::: {.lesson-beat}

The **advantage function** is
$$A_\pi(s, a) \;=\; q_\pi(s, a) - v_\pi(s).$$

The **advantage actor-critic (A2C)** update on a transition $(s, a, r, s')$
uses the 1-step TD error as an advantage estimator:

- **Critic ($\hat v$):**
  $w \leftarrow w + \alpha_w\,\delta\,\nabla_w \hat v(s; w),
  \quad \delta = r + \gamma \hat v(s'; w) - \hat v(s; w).$
- **Actor:**
  $\theta \leftarrow \theta + \alpha_\theta\,\nabla_\theta \log \pi(a \mid s; \theta)\,\delta.$

**Baseline invariance** (the underlying lemma): for any $b(s)$,
$\mathbb E_{a \sim \pi}[\nabla \log \pi(a \mid s)\, b(s)] = 0$.

:::

## Apply

### Worked example — A2C on a 2-action MDP

State $s_1$, two actions $\{a_1, a_2\}$. Suppose $q_\pi(s_1, a_1) = 12$,
$q_\pi(s_1, a_2) = 8$, and the policy is $\pi(a_1) = 0.5,\ \pi(a_2) = 0.5$.

Compute $v_\pi(s_1) = 0.5 \cdot 12 + 0.5 \cdot 8 = 10$. Then
$A_\pi(s_1, a_1) = 12 - 10 = 2$, $A_\pi(s_1, a_2) = 8 - 10 = -2$.

Notice $\sum_a \pi(a) A(s, a) = 0.5 \cdot 2 + 0.5 \cdot (-2) = 0$. ✓

The actor's gradient using $A$ has magnitude proportional to 2, not 10.
Variance drops by a factor of $(10/2)^2 = 25$ — without changing the
*sign* of the gradient (which still pushes toward $a_1$ and away from
$a_2$, the right direction).

If we had used $q$ unmodified (QAC), the gradient would be the same in
expectation but ~5× noisier per sample.

### Practice — Warm

**W1.** State the advantage function in symbols.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$A_\pi(s, a) = q_\pi(s, a) - v_\pi(s)$.
</details>

**W2.** What is $\sum_a \pi(a \mid s) A_\pi(s, a)$ for any $s$? Why?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$0$. Because $\sum_a \pi(a \mid s) q_\pi(s, a) = v_\pi(s)$ by definition,
so $\sum_a \pi(a \mid s) [q_\pi(s, a) - v_\pi(s)] = v_\pi(s) - v_\pi(s) = 0$.
</details>

**W3.** Is the A2C actor gradient unbiased (assuming $\hat v$ is exact)?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**Yes** — the TD error with exact $\hat v = v_\pi$ has expected value
$A_\pi(s_t, a_t)$, and substituting this unbiased estimate of $A$ for $A$
in the policy gradient theorem keeps the gradient unbiased. With learned
$\hat v$, there is bias (the critic isn't exact), same as in QAC.
</details>

### Practice — Working

**P1.** Why does A2C only need a $\hat v$-critic, not a $\hat q$-critic?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
The TD error with $\hat v$ already estimates the advantage.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Because $\delta_t = r_t + \gamma \hat v(s_{t+1}) - \hat v(s_t) \approx
A_\pi(s_t, a_t)$ in expectation. So a single $\hat v$ network gives both
the value baseline *and* the bootstrap target — half as many parameters as
a $\hat q$ critic.
</details>

**P2.** Show that the variance of the actor's gradient under A2C is
generally lower than under QAC. Give the intuition.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
QAC's actor uses $\hat q$ (large positive numbers); A2C uses
$\hat A = \hat q - \hat v$ (centred around zero).
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\text{Var}(\nabla \log \pi \cdot \hat q) \approx \mathbb E[\hat q^2]
\|\nabla \log \pi\|^2$.
$\text{Var}(\nabla \log \pi \cdot \hat A) \approx \mathbb E[\hat A^2]
\|\nabla \log \pi\|^2$.
Since $\hat A$ has *smaller magnitude* than $\hat q$ on average (the mean
is subtracted), $\mathbb E[\hat A^2] \ll \mathbb E[\hat q^2]$. So variance
is generally smaller for A2C — often by 1–3 orders of magnitude on real
tasks.
</details>

**P3.** A practitioner uses an **n-step return** for the advantage:
$\hat A_t^{(n)} = r_t + \gamma r_{t+1} + \cdots + \gamma^{n-1} r_{t+n-1} +
\gamma^n \hat v(s_{t+n}) - \hat v(s_t)$. State the bias-variance trade as
$n$ grows.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Compare $n = 1$ (1-step TD) and $n = T$ (full MC return).
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
- $n = 1$: lowest variance, highest bias (depends most on $\hat v$).
- $n = T$ (full return): highest variance, lowest bias (depends least on
  $\hat v$ — really only on $\hat v(s_t)$).
- Intermediate $n$: smooth trade. **GAE** (Generalised Advantage
  Estimation; Schulman et al. 2016) is a clever exponentially-weighted
  combination that interpolates the trade-off with one parameter $\lambda
  \in [0, 1]$. Most modern AC methods (PPO included) use GAE.
</details>

### Practice — Stretch

**S1.** Show that for the optimal policy $\pi^*$, the advantage at every
$(s, a)$ is $\le 0$ for actions in $\arg\max_a q_{\pi^*}(s, a)$, and
$\le 0$ for *all* actions when the optimal policy is unique.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$v_{\pi^*}(s) = \max_a q_{\pi^*}(s, a)$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
For a unique optimal policy, $v_{\pi^*}(s) = \max_a q_{\pi^*}(s, a) =
q_{\pi^*}(s, a^*)$ where $a^*$ is the unique optimal action. So
$A_{\pi^*}(s, a^*) = 0$ and $A_{\pi^*}(s, a) = q_{\pi^*}(s, a) - q_{\pi^*}(s, a^*)
\le 0$ for all $a \ne a^*$. **All advantages are non-positive.** This makes
sense: at optimality, no action is better-than-average, because the average
*is* the best action's value.

If the actor's gradient ever drives $\theta$ to a $\pi$ where every
sampled $A \le 0$ (and $= 0$ only for selected actions), the policy is
optimal — and the gradient stops changing $\theta$.
</details>

**S2.** Construct a 2-state, 2-action MDP and verify by hand that
subtracting $v_\pi(s)$ from $q_\pi(s, a)$ does not change the policy
gradient direction.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Pick simple numbers; compute $\sum_a \pi(a) \nabla \log \pi(a) q$ and
$\sum_a \pi(a) \nabla \log \pi(a) [q - v]$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Take state-independent softmax with $\theta = (\theta_1, \theta_2)$,
$\pi = (0.5, 0.5)$ at $\theta = 0$. Suppose $q(s, a_1) = 12,\ q(s, a_2) = 8$.
$\nabla \log \pi(a_1) = e_1 - \pi = (0.5, -0.5)$;
$\nabla \log \pi(a_2) = (-0.5, 0.5)$.

Without baseline: $\sum_a \pi(a) \nabla \log \pi(a) q = 0.5(0.5,-0.5)(12) +
0.5(-0.5, 0.5)(8) = (3, -3) + (-2, 2) = (1, -1)$.

With baseline $v = 10$: $\sum_a \pi(a) \nabla \log \pi(a) (q - v) = 0.5(0.5,
-0.5)(2) + 0.5(-0.5, 0.5)(-2) = (0.5, -0.5) + (0.5, -0.5) = (1, -1)$. ✓ Same
direction. Same magnitude. Variance per sample is what differs.
</details>

::: {.common-confusion}
### Common confusions

1. **"Subtracting $v$ changes the gradient."** No — only its variance.
   The expectation is unchanged. This is the baseline-invariance lemma in
   action.
2. **"Action-dependent baselines work too."** No — they bias the gradient
   (Lesson 34, P2). Only state-only (or time-only) baselines preserve
   unbiasedness.
3. **"Advantage requires a $\hat q$ network."** No — the 1-step TD error
   $\delta = r + \gamma \hat v(s') - \hat v(s)$ is an unbiased estimate of
   $A_\pi$ when $\hat v$ is exact, and only requires a $\hat v$ critic.
:::

### Takeaways

1. **$A_\pi(s, a) = q_\pi(s, a) - v_\pi(s)$.** The "extra value" of taking
   $a$ over the policy's average action.
2. **Baseline invariance:** subtracting any $b(s)$ does not bias the
   policy gradient. Choosing $b = v_\pi$ gives $A_\pi$, the
   variance-optimal state-only baseline (in the limit of accurate $\hat v$).
3. **A2C** uses $\delta_t = r_t + \gamma \hat v(s_{t+1}) - \hat v(s_t)$ as
   an advantage estimator. One critic, one actor, the lowest-variance
   simple PG method. The skeleton of A3C, PPO, IMPALA, SAC, and most modern
   on-policy deep RL.

### Self-Assessment

1. Could you state the baseline invariance lemma and verify it on a
   2-action MDP?
2. Did you derive the TD error → advantage relationship in *Discover* P4?
3. Could you sketch A2C pseudo-code?

- **3 yes:** continue to [Lesson 37 — Off-Policy AC](../37-off-policy-actor-critic/index.qmd).
- **2 yes:** redo P2 and S2.
- **0–1 yes:** re-derive Discover P4.

## Connect

### Prerequisites used

- **[mfrl.actor-critic-qac](../35-actor-critic-qac/index.qmd)** — the
  actor-critic skeleton with $\hat q$.
- **[mfrl.policy-gradient-theorem](../33-policy-gradient-theorem/index.qmd)** —
  the baseline-invariance lemma proved in S2.

### What this unlocks

- **[Off-policy AC](../37-off-policy-actor-critic/index.qmd)** — extend
  A2C to behaviour ≠ target via importance sampling.
- **[DDPG](../38-deterministic-actor-critic/index.qmd)** — the
  deterministic-policy continuous-control variant.

### Where this matters

::: {.applied-callout}
**AI / ML.** Advantage estimation is universal in modern policy-based RL.
PPO, A3C, SAC, IMPALA, VMPO, MPO — all use some form of advantage
estimator (often Generalised Advantage Estimation). The baseline
invariance lemma is the theoretical hook that makes advantage estimation
*free* — variance reduction with no bias cost.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Sister synthesis lesson.** [`t7.rl-theory`](../../tier-7-masters-tracks/06-rl-theory/index.qmd)
gives the single-paragraph view of reinforcement learning that this
track expands into 38 lessons.
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Author's notes

- Source: Shiyu Zhao, *Mathematical Foundations of Reinforcement Learning*
  (Springer 2025), §10 (advantage actor-critic). Original references:
  Mnih et al. (2016), *Asynchronous Methods for Deep Reinforcement
  Learning* (A3C); Schulman et al. (2016), *High-Dimensional Continuous
  Control Using Generalized Advantage Estimation*.
- Touchstones honoured: Russian school (Discover walks the learner from
  baseline invariance to advantage to TD-error-as-advantage); Better
  Explained "Aha!" framing (the key insight that subtracting the average
  is free variance reduction).
:::
